EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major Bering sea storm and the associated front that extended to the Gulf of Alaska produced high winds across the Aleutians and blizzard conditions from the Pribilof Islands to the Bering Sea coast and high wind heavy snow and blizzard conditions across south central Alaska and Prince William Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cordova Boat Harbor weather system observed an 81 KT gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major Bering sea storm and the associated front that extended to the Gulf of Alaska produced high winds across the Aleutians and blizzard conditions from the Pribilof Islands to the Bering Sea coast and high wind heavy snow and blizzard conditions across south central Alaska and Prince William Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters in the Susitna Valley reported snow fall totals as high as 37 inches for this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense north Pacific storm moved into the central Bering Sea overnight December 3rd. This storm produced high wind across the Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula to the Pribilof Islands as well as snow and blizzard conditions in the Pribilof Islands.

A storm in the northern Gulf of Alaska produced heavy snow and localized strong wind. Snow fall totaled over 7 feet in Thompson Pass from December 14th through December 16th. Along with the snow gusty wind created blizzard conditions through the pass. Snow fell in the Kachemak Bay area with this storm and combined with locally gusty wind around the Kachemak Bay area to produce blizzard conditions.
An intense Bering Sea Storm produced localized high wind along the Kuskokwim Delta and Bristol Bay coast of Alaska. The peak wind was 78 mph in this region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Platinum measured a peak gust of 68 KT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense Bering Sea Storm produced localized high wind along the Kuskokwim Delta and Bristol Bay coast of Alaska. The peak wind was 78 mph in this region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Platinum measured a peak gust of 68 KT. Based upon this observation it is estimated gust were at least this high in Kuskokwim Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm in Prince William Sound produced heavy snow and strong wind in Thompson Pass that resulted in a blizzard through the pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thompson Pass Department of Transportation reported blizzard conditions through Thompson Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major Bering sea storm and the associated front that extended to the Gulf of Alaska produced high winds across the Aleutians and blizzard conditions from the Pribilof Islands to the Bering Sea coast and high wind heavy snow and blizzard conditions across south central Alaska and Prince William Sound.

A low pressure center and the associated weather front over the Yukon Territory of Canada produced a local setup for heavy snow over the Upper Koyukuk Valley as moisture overran the cold air in place. The FAA Weather Observers at Bettles recorded 10.2 inches of snow from the 17th to the 18th, with 9 inches falling from the mid  afternoon of the 17th through the mid afternoon of the 18th.

A strong pressure and temperature gradient along the North Gulf Coast between a strong Arctic high and Gulf of Alaska low produced high wind through the gaps in the Chugach Mountains. The peak measured wind was 86 mph through Thompson Pass.

A strong pressure and temperature gradient along the North Gulf Coast between a strong Arctic high and Gulf of Alaska low produced high wind through the gaps in the Chugach Mountains. The peak measured wind was 86 mph through Thompson Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind speed out of the Copper River Delta is estimated based upon the pressure gradient and temperature gradient and reported wind gusts through Thompson Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm and associated front moved from the north Pacific toward the central Aleutians. Strong wind and snow in advance of the front produced blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based upon available observations it is estimated that blizzard conditions were occurring in this region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm and associated front moved from the north Pacific toward the central Aleutians. Strong wind and snow in advance of the front produced blizzard conditions.
A low in the northern Gulf of Alaska produced strong northwest wind across southern Cook Inlet. Snow along with the wind resulted in a Blizzard in the Homer and Kachemak Bay area.

A strong storm in the Gulf of Alaska produced high wind around the northwest quadrant of the storm. Wind gust peaked at 98 mph in Dutch Harbor and were around 70 mph through the channeled terrain of the Alaska Peninsula.

A strong storm in the Gulf of Alaska produced high wind around the northwest quadrant of the storm. Wind gust peaked at 98 mph in Dutch Harbor and were around 70 mph through the channeled terrain of the Alaska Peninsula.
A storm in the northern Gulf of Alaska produced strong north wind and snow across the Chugach Mountains that resulted in a blizzard through Thompson Pass.
Strong north flow along the Bering Sea coast produced frequent snow showers and strong northwest wind that resulted in blizzard conditions along the coast of the Kuskokwim Delta.
A strong north Pacific storm moved across the Aleutians on the 18th and 19th producing strong wind in Shemya. The peak wind was 74 mph at Shemya.
A front moved across the Pribilof Islands on the 19th producing strong wind and snow that resulted in blizzard conditions.

A strong pressure gradient developed between a 1058 mb high across northeast Alaska and low pressure in the Bering Sea.  The strong pressure gradient produced high winds and blizzard conditions along part of the Chukchi Sea coast during the afternoon and evening hours of the 21st.  White-out conditions in heavy blowing and drifting snow were observed at Point Hope during the afternoon, and the visibility remained one quarter mile or less at times into the evening. Both Point Hope and Kivalina observed wind gusts to 50 mph/58 kt.
A front moved into the eastern Bering Sea producing strong northeast wind and snow across the Pribilof Islands that resulted in a blizzard.
An intense front moved through the western Aleutians the evening of February 7th. Hurricane force wind gusts were associated with this front as it moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ship reports of sustained 60 KT were observed in the region. Estimate gusts over 70 KT with this front based upon the ship observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moved to the Pribilof Islands February 8th. Strong wind and snow with this front produced blizzard conditions in the Pribilof Islands.

A cold front moved from western Alaska to south central Alaska February 18th and 19th. Strong moist flow in advance of the front into the Susitna Valley produced 8 to 12 inches of snow in the Susitna Valley on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters in the Susitna Valley reported 8 to 12 inches of snow and possibly more.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moved to the Bering Sea coast on the 20th and 21st producing strong wind and snow that resulted in a blizzard along the Kuskokwim Delta.
A strong north Pacific storm produced high wind over the central Aleutians on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Base on surface observations and the synoptic analysis wind is estimated to have reached 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low brought high wind and snow to the Bering Sea coast the evening of February 25th that produced blizzard conditions.
A strong low brought high wind and snow to the Bering Sea coast the evening of February 25th that produced blizzard conditions.
A front moved across the Pribilof Islands producing strong wind and snow that resulted in a blizzard.
A strong low in the gulf spread snow and high wind into the Chugach Mountains. The combined wind and snow resulted in blizzard conditions in Thompson Pass.

Blizzard conditions occurred in the western Aleutians on the 8th then in the Pribilof Islands on the 9th. The front associated with this storm produced snow in the Portage Valley area along with strong wind that resulted in a blizzard.

A strong storm and front produced a blizzard the evening of April 21st along the coast of the Kuskokwim Delta.
A storm moved through the western Aleutians producing strong northeast wind and snow that resulted in a blizzard.
A strong low and front moved into the eastern Bering Sea produced strong wind in advance of the front along with snow that resulted in a blizzard.
A strong low moved through the central Aleutians producing strong northwest wind and snow in the western Aleutians producing a blizzard.
A strong low moved into the eastern Bering Sea producing strong wind in advance of its front. The strong wind and snow produced blizzard conditions along the Kuskokwim Delta coast.
A strong storm moved through the central Aleutians March 7th. The front pushed through the eastern Aleutians producing a brief period of high wind in Dutch Harbor.

The previous nights snowfall of eleven inches set the stage for this blizzard March 7th. Strong north wind on the 7th re suspended the snow producing blizzard conditions through Thompson Pass on the 7th.
Strong northwest wind and snow around the northwest quadrant of a Gulf of Alaska low produced blizzard conditions across the Alaska Peninsula.
Strong north wind and snow produced blizzard conditions in Turnagain Pass to Seward.

A strong low moved into the northern Gulf of Alaska producing heavy snow and strong wind across Kodiak Island. Snow fall began Sunday before mixing with rain then back to snow totaling around 20 inches over the two day period. The heavy snow and strong northwest wind produced blizzard conditions and substantial drifting snow.

An intense storm moved into the Eastern Bering Sea Saturday. This storm packed high wind and snow as it moved across the Alaska Peninsula to the Bering Sea coast. Strong wind peaked at 100 KT at Saint George in the midst of the Blizzard on the 28th. The strong wind moved into south central Alaska Saturday night and Sunday along with moderate to heavy snow fall. Whittier reported 2 to 2.5 feet of snow with this event. Portage Valley experienced high wind and heavy snow resulting in a white out blizzard.

An unseasonably intense 974 MB storm for July moved into the Bristol Bay area on the 21st of July. The associated front pushed across south central Alaska producing strong wind across the Chugach Mountains through Portage Pass.

A tropically connected storm resulted in heavy rain over the Kenai Peninsula that produced flooding in the Seward area. The approach to the bridge to Lowell Point washed out and a land slide at a tunnel at mile 11 shut down the Alaska Railroad.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River gages in Seward exceeded flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Smoke from nearby wildfires moved into the Fairbanks area during the morning of the 30th and remained below 1 mile visibility until the early evening. The thick smoke forced the cancellation of a number of outdoor activities, including all Fairbanks Youth Football events, all Fairbanks Youth Soccer Association games, Golden Heart Softball Association games, a Goldpanner/Athletes in Action double-header baseball game, and a cycling time trial. The Railbelt Fire south of Nenana and the Woodriver 1 Fire south of Fairbanks were the contributors of the dense smoke.
The Skilak Glacial Lake released resulting in flooding along the Kenai River from the outburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reconnaissance of the Skilak Lake by the Kenai Peninsula Borough revealed the glacial dam release.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tazlina and Nelchina glacial dam lakes release beginning August 25th. The subsequent rises in water levels produced flooding along the Tazlina River near the Richardson Highway on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pilot reports from the area stated the glacial dam lakes of the Tazlina and Nelchina glacier streams had released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overflow on the Tisuk River created washouts on the Nome-Teller Highway at mile 25 and 48 on or about May 20th. The Kougarok Road at mile 38 also received a washout during the same period of time. Estimated Road Damages are $8,000.

Water levels rose on the Colville River at Colville as snow melt from the Brooks Range entered the river.  The airport runway was under water, and water moved as high as the foundation of the warehouse near the airport.  Many low lying areas were under water, but no structures were flooded.  When the water receded the airstrip was eroded in a few spots, and the flooding caused approximately $1500 in damages.
A strong storm moved in along the north Gulf Coast producing high wind in the Cordova area the night of September 10th. The peak wind reported at the Cordova Boat Harbor was 96 mph.

A 986 mb low in the northern Bering Sea and a 1027 mb high across interior Alaska produced a strong pressure gradient that produced|a period of blizzard conditions on Saint Lawrence on the 2nd. ||Zone 213: Blizzard conditions were observed at Gambell during the morning and early afternoon hours on the 2nd.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow. A peak wind gust of 47 mph/41kt was observed at the Gambell AWOS.

Temperatures of 25 to 30 below combined with a northeast wind of 20 to 30 mph to produce wind chills as low as 64 below at Point Lay and 61 below at Atqasuk during the morning and early afternoon hours on the 29th.

A 975 mb low in the northern Gulf of Alaska combined with a 1048 mb high across central Russia to produce strong winds and blizzard conditions along portions of the Chukchi Sea coast from the early morning hours on the 26th through the morning hours on the 27th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less at Kivalina in snow and blowing snow.  A peak wind gust of 61 mph/53kt was observed at the Kivalina ASOS.

A series of intense tropically connected storms moved into the eastern Bering Sea beginning January 13th. The storms pushed the warm tropical air over the existing deep arctic air that had been over Alaska since the end of December 2008. High wind, snow, and freezing rain occurred throughout the south central and southwest regions of Alaska while strong north wind and snow produced blizzard conditions in the Pribilof Islands. Wind at higher elevations of the Chugach Mountains exceeded 120 mph. The upper hillside of the Anchorage area had several spotter reports of wind around 110 mph and wind reached 50 mph in east Anchorage. Freezing rain created chaos across the south central region on the 14th and 15th resulting in many vehicles sliding off the road and numerous roll over accidents. Windows were blown out of a local McDonald's and some vehicles. The rapid warming combined with heavy rain resulted in localized flooding in the Anchorage area, Valdez and Girdwood.

Two powerful north Pacific storms moved across the eastern Aleutians beginning December 4th continuing through December 7th. The storms produced winds of 125 mph across the eastern Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula and to 70 to 85 mph across the Bristol Bay area and to around 75 mph in the Pribilof Islands. Extensive damage was reported in the community of Unalaska and Dutch Harbor and significant damage was reported in King Cove and in the communities of St Paul and St George. The APL crane in Dutch Harbor was toppled by the extreme wind.

A 968 mb low near Adak at 900 am AKST on the 19th deepened to 960 mb between the Pribilof Islands and Saint Matthew Island by 3 am AKST on the 20th. A second stronger low developed near the Alaska Peninsula by 3 am AKST on the 21st, and deepened to 952 mb near Saint Matthew Island by 3 pm AKST on the 21st. The first low brought a period of blizzard conditions to Gambell on the morning of the 20th. The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow. The wind gusted as high as 42kt/48 mph at the Gambell AWOS. The second low brought snow and reduced the visibility to one quarter of a mile at times, but blizzard conditions were not observed. ||The second low brought a period of blizzard conditions to Selawik on the morning of the 21st. The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow. The wind gusted as high as 35 kt/40 mph at the Selawik AWOS. The visibility at Kotzebue during this event briefly dropped to one quarter of a mile, but during most of the event was one half mile or better. The wind gusted as high as 41kt/47 mph at the Kotzebue ASOS.

The combination of a deep upper low in the Bering Sea and a large upper ridge across eastern Alaska brought a period of strong Chinook flow across the Alaska Range.  The wind at one of the Healy CWOP sites gusted to 50 kt/58 mph, and there was an unconfirmed report of wind gusts as high as 74 kt/85 mph in the Healy area.  The report of 85 mph winds seemed reasonable given the meteorological conditions. There was a peak wind gust of 63 kt/72 mph at the Antler Creek DOT site at Mile post 244 along the Parks Highway.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm center developed to 946 MB early on the morning of 11/05. Strong winds accompanied an associated front as it moved north through through Southeast Alaska on 11/05. A developing swell from the storm increased the amount water moving onshore over the coastal areas simultaneously during the time of the highest tides of November for Southeast Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated storm surge of roughly 1 to 3 feet above the astronomical high tide.  Outside seas were 18 FT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Chinook produced a period of high winds in and near the Alaska Range. In the Upper Kuskokwim Valley the wind frequently gusted over 70 mph at Farewell lake, with peak wind gusts of 78 mph during the afternoon and early evening of the 5th.  In the Denali Weather zone, high winds occurred at the Antler Creek Dot site at MP 244 along the Parks Highway where a peak wind gust of 71 mph was observed at noon.

CZ_NAME: NE. SLOPES OF THE ERN AK RNG
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed south of the Kenai Peninsula on the morning of the 14th, and tracked to the Prince William Sound where the low remained quasi-stationary and slowly deepened on the 15th into the 16th. The low brought heavy snowfall to parts of the Eastern Alaska Range.  A total of 26 inches of snow fell at Isabel Pass, which included 20 inches of snow in 24 hours from approximately 5 pm AKST on the 14th through 5 pm AKST on the 15th. The Co-operative Weather Observer at Bartell Creek, seven miles east of Mentasta Lake reported 14.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 900 AKST on the 15th.

The storms pushed the warm tropical air over the existing deep arctic air that had been over Alaska since the end of December 2008. High wind, snow, and freezing rain occurred throughout the south central and southwest regions of Alaska while strong north wind and snow produced blizzard conditions in the Pribilof Islands. Wind at higher elevations of the Chugach Mountains exceeded 120 mph. The upper hillside of the Anchorage area had several spotter reports of wind around 110 mph and wind reached 50 mph in east Anchorage. Freezing rain created chaos across the south central region on the 14th and 15th resulting in many vehicles sliding off the road and numerous roll over accidents. Windows were blown out of a local McDonald's and some vehicles. The rapid warming combined with heavy rain resulted in localized flooding in the Anchorage area, Valdez and Girdwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple observation sites on the Delta reported blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low from the North Pacific Ocean deepened  to 952 MB near Middleton Island on the morning of Saturday 11/14. The associated front moved onshore and through Southeast Alaska by Saturday evening.  This system brought strong winds and heavy snow to many regions of Southeast Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hyder COOP observer measured 1.45 inches of water equivalent on 11/14 and 0.64 inches on 11/15. Although the snow dumped on the Misty Fjords area, much of the snow was compacted and washed away when mixed or changing to rain. Only 2 inches of snowfall resulted in the town of Hyder, but outlying areas received feet of new snow.

CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low from the North Pacific Ocean deepened  to 952 MB near Middleton Island on the morning of Saturday 11/14. The associated front moved onshore and through Southeast Alaska by Saturday evening.  This system brought strong winds and heavy snow to many regions of Southeast Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sitka ASOS had several peak winds of 49 KT during the afternoon of 11/14. NCEP analyses showed that even stronger headland winds to 80 MPH were possible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two powerful north Pacific storms moved across the eastern Aleutians beginning December 4th continuing through December 7th. The storms produced winds of 125 mph across the eastern Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula and to 70 to 85 mph across the Bristol Bay area and to around 75 mph in the Pribilof Islands. Extensive damage was reported in the community of Unalaska and Dutch Harbor and significant damage was reported in King Cove and in the communities of St Paul and St George. The APL crane in Dutch Harbor was toppled by the extreme wind.

A 949 mb low across the Alaska Peninsula on the morning of the 16th tracked into the Norton Sound early on the morning of the 17th, and weakened to 978 mb by Noon on the 17th.  The low re-developed across the Arctic Ocean during the afternoon of the 17th, and tracked to north of Banks Island as a 985 mb low on the morning of the 18th.  The storm brought high winds, heavy snow, blizzard conditions and freezing rain to much of the west coast as well as the arctic coast.||Zone 201: Heavy snow and blizzard conditions were observed at Point Lay during the late evening hours of the 16th. The visibility at the Point Lay AWOS was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile at times. ||Zone 205: Heavy snow and blizzard conditions likely occurred in spots across the Northwestern Brooks Range based on similar conditions that were observed in Northeastern Brooks Range. The wind at the Ikpikpuk River automatic Stream gauge was sustained at 25 to 40 mph and occasionally as high as 46kt/53 mph during the afternoon and evening hours of the 17th. ||Zone 206: Heavy snow and blizzard conditions likely occurred in spots across the Northeastern Brooks Range. The wind at Anaktuvuk Pass AWOS gusted to 48 kt/55 mph. The DOT had a travel advisory in effect for heavy snowfall with drifting and blowing snow and near zero visibility. In fact, conditions were so bad in the vicinity of Atigun Pass that the DOT suspended the use of maintenance equipment on the morning of the 17th due to avalanche slides and snow drifts blocking the Dalton Highway. ||Zone 207: Heavy snow and blizzard conditions occurred at Point Hope and Kivalina. The Point Hope AWOS reported visibilities of one quarter mile or less from the afternoon on the evening of the 16th through the evening of the 17th along with wind gusts to 35kt/40 mph. The Kivalina ASOS reported two hours of heavy snow during the evening hours of the 16th, with the visibility one quarter of a mile or less along with wind gusts to 36kt/41 mph. The heavy snow changed over to freezing rain which likely accumulated to one tenth of an inch. ||Zone 208: Heavy snow was observed by the Noatak cooperative observer during the afternoon of the 16th through the afternoon of the 17th. Six inches of snow was observed from noon on the 16th through 4 am on the 17th, with a storm total of 8.5 inches. In addition to the snow, a south wind gusted to 35kt/40 mph at the Noatak AWOS and likely produced considerable blowing and drifting snow. In addition, temperatures climbed above freezing for a time during the early morning hours on the 17th at the Noatak AWOS and the Kiana RAWS, and likely produced freezing rain with accumulations of around one tenth of an inch in spots. ||Zone 209: Periods of heavy snow that reduced the visibility to one quarter of a mile were observed at Kotzebue on the evening of the 16th. Although only 3.5 inches of snow was observed at the WSO in Kotzebue, as warmer air moved into the area the snow changed to freezing rain during the evening hours on the 16th, and accumulated approximately one tenth of an inch. As the storm system began to pull away from the area on the afternoon of the 17th the freezing rain changed back to snow. The visibility was reduced to one quarter of a mile at times in snow and blowing snow. The wind gusted to 37kt/43 mph at the Kotzebue ASOS during the afternoon of the 17th. ||Zone 210: Blizzard conditions were observed along the coast during the afternoon hours of the 17th. The Deering ASOS reported visibilities of one quarter mile or less in heavy snow at times. A south wind of 30 to 45 mph was observed, with wind gusts as high as 53kt/61 mph. Similar conditions were observed at Buckland. The Buckland AWOS reported visibilities of zero at times, and wind gusts as high as 57kt/66 mph. ||Zone 211: Blizzard conditions and heavy snow were observed at Nome during the afternoon and evening hours of the 16th. A total of 8.3 inches of snow was observed at the Nome Weather Service Office. The visibility was also frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow, and occasionally to zero. Temperatures briefly climbed above freezing during the evening hours of the 16th into the early morning hours of the 17th with freezing rain. The wind gusted to 44 kt/51 mph at the Nome ASOS. Blizzard conditions were also observed at Golovin AWOS on the morning hour of the 17th. The visibility was reduced to zero, and the wind gusted as high as 52kt/60 mph. ||Zone 212: Heavy snow and blizzard conditions were observed at the Koyuk AWOS during the morning and afternoon hours of the 17th. The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less, and occasionally to zero in snow and blowing snow. The wind gusted as high as 42kt/48 mph. The wind gusted as high as 65kt/75 mph at the St. Michael AWSS during the early morning hours of the 17th, and likely produced blizzard conditions. ||Zone 213: Heavy snow and blizzard conditions were observed at Teller along the Bering Strait coast. The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 16th at the Teller AWSS, and the wind gusted to 32 kt/37 mph. On Saint Lawrence Island, the wind gusted to 47kt/54 mph at the Gambell AWOS and blizzard conditions likely occurred. ||Zone 215: High winds were observed at the Marshall AWSS during the early morning hours of the 17th. Wind gusts to 55kt/63mph were observed. Periods of snow were also observed, but it is unknown how much snow accumulated. The Anvik AWOS briefly had visibilities reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow during the early morning hours of the 17th. ||Zone 216: A cooperative observer in Galena reported trees and power lines down during the early morning hours of the 17th. Sustained winds of 50 mph were observed, with a peak wind gust of 64 mph at 331 AKDT. Wind gusts to 56kt/64 mph were observed at the Poorman Raws and to 55kt/63 mph at the Koyukuk Raws during the morning hours of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm center developed to 946 MB early on the morning of 11/05. Strong winds accompanied an associated front as it moved north through through Southeast Alaska on 11/05. A developing swell from the storm increased the amount water moving onshore over the coastal areas simultaneously during the time of the highest tides of November for Southeast Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tide gauges recorded water levels of 1.5 to 2 ft above the astronomical high tides which were amongst the highest of the months. High water levels in Sitka Harbor and the gusty winds going on resulted in a couple of boats coming loose and beaching themselves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Chinook developed as a ridge of high pressure developed across the eastern Pacific and a trough of low pressure developed across eastern Russia.  This produced a period of high winds in the Alaska Range as well as parts of the Deltana Flats and the Upper Tanana Valley.  The most significant event occurred in Delta Junction (zone 223) about a mile from the Delta River. The high winds that were estimated between 80 and 95 mph blew down trees and as well as the roof of an occupied cabin. The south side of the roof was lifted off the cabin and ripped apart, landing in a pile more than 100 feet from the cabin. The other half of the roof lifted off the cabin and flipped, landing as a complete unit about 30 feet from the cabin. When the roof was blown off the cabin the wood stove in the cabin was moved about 2 feet and the interior stove pipe was lying on the floor several feet away. Estimate damage to this residence is $15,000. The high winds knocked out power and phone service in the area, which was restored to most homes later in the day. At one point during the morning, much of the city of Delta Junction was without power. At Whitestone Farms near Delta Junction, many trees were reported blown down. Minor damage was sustained to a building and a vehicle when a tree fell onto both, estimated damage $1700, a window was broken when a tree branch came through it, estimated damage $300, and metal roofing came off a portion of a building, estimate damage $1000. In Denali National Park and Preserve (zone 225), a number of trees were reported blown down.||The highest wind gusts that were measured or observed were:||Zone 220: Eagle Summit Meteorburst: Measured 68 mph/59 kt.|Zone 223: Delta Junction: Estimated 80 to 95 mph. The Whitestone Farms Davis instruments anemometer showed a winds speed just over 78 knots/90 mph before it stopped reporting. |Zone 224: NWS Spotter near Robertson River on Alaska Highway: Estimated 60 mph/52kt. |Zone 225: Antler Creek DOT RWIS (on the Parks Highway MP 244) measured 74 mph/64 kt.|Zone 226: Texas Condo: Measured 80 mph/69 kt.|          Washington Range: Measured 77 mph/67 kt.|          OP 12A: Measured 84 mph/73 kt.||The observations in zone 226 are all part of the Fort Greely mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two powerful north Pacific storms moved across the eastern Aleutians beginning December 4th continuing through December 7th. The storms produced winds of 125 mph across the eastern Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula and to 70 to 85 mph across the Bristol Bay area and to around 75 mph in the Pribilof Islands. Extensive damage was reported in the community of Unalaska and Dutch Harbor and significant damage was reported in King Cove and in the communities of St Paul and St George. The APL crane in Dutch Harbor was toppled by the extreme wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The King Cove Police Chief reported that a public anemometer measured a peak gust of 109 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hoonah public works water plant (COOP) observed 13.5 inches of new snowfall between 0800 AKST and 1730 AKST on 11/16. Snowfall tapered off during the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front was over northern Lynn Canal on 11/23, with a trough moving onshore the northeast Gulf Coast and northern SE Alaska. Warm, moist air was rising aloft over the cold air in place bringing heavy snow the Haines Borough and portions of Lynn Canal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs observed 5.6 inches of new snow on the afternoon of 11/23.  It is highly likely there was higher amounts that occurred along the highway in the Chilkat River valley.  Based on observations at the Haines ASOS, the snow mainly fell up the highway during a period of 8 hours or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures held steady near 32F as cooler air continued to drain down the Chilkat River valley, while a frontal band moved north across the Southeast Alaska panhandle with warm, moist air aloft on 11/25 and 11/26.  Overrunning heavy snow persisted during that time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at the Haines Custom station measured 14 inches of new snowfall|for 24 hours ending at 0700 on 11/26. Based on surface observations in the area, the heavier snowfall fell during the day into the next morning. The snowfall was wet and changed to rain the morning of the 26th.

An intense north Pacific storm moved across the Alaska Peninsula October 9th and 10th. The storm produced high wind across Kodiak Island and the Bristol Bay area. Along with the Strong wind heavy rain fell across Kodiak Island and the southern Kenai Peninsula. The city of Kodiak measured 7.5 inches of rain on the 9th and 10th. The heavy rain resulted in minor small stream flooding and a few mud slides.
An intense low and associated front moved into the Gulf of Alaska on the 22nd. Wind in advance of the front reached 75 mph through Hinchinbrook Entrance in southern Prince William Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Seabulk Arctic measured 65 KT wind in Hinchinbrook Entrance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure in the interior of Alaska combined with a low in the northern Gulf of Alaska produced a strong pressure gradient that resulted in high wind in Valdez. The peak measured gust was 79 mph in Valdez during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valdez Handar observed a peak gust of 69 KT. The Thompson Pass road weather information system observed gusts to 70 mph during this wind storm.

Strong northwest wind and snow around the northwest side of the low produced a blizzard in the Pribilof Islands.

A 965 mb low across the eastern Aleutians re-developed across southwest Alaska on the evening of the 14th, and lifted north to northwest Alaska on the morning of the 15th.  The low brought heavy snow and freezing rain to a large portion of northern Alaska.  The snow combined with high winds to produce blizzard conditions in parts of the Brooks Range. ||Zone 206: There were DOT reports of near blizzard conditions at Atigun Pass along the Dalton Highway, and based on surrounding observations there was likely 6 inches or more of snow in spots along the Dalton Highway and across the higher elevations of the Brooks Range. ||Zone 208: Heavy snow was observed at Noatak.  The cooperative observer at Noatak observed 6.5 inches of snow from the morning of the 14th through the morning of the 15th. ||Zone 209: Heavy snow was observed at Kotzebue, and likely occurred at Selawik. Seven inches of snow was observed at Kotzebue. ||Zone 210: Based on the observations from the Deering and Buckland ASOS's, along with the measured snowfall in Kotzebue, it is estimated that 7 to 9 inches of snow likely accumulated. ||Zone 212: Based on the observation of 7 inches of snow at Kotzebue along with the visibility restrictions on the Koyuk AWOS, it is estimated that 8 to 12 inches of snow fell in the upslope areas across the Nulato Hills. Temperatures at Unalakleet briefly climbed above freezing early on the morning of the 15th, and this may have produced areas of freezing rain along the eastern Norton Sound coast. ||Zone 215: Based on the NWS observation of 8.7 inches of snow at McGrath, it is estimated that 8 to 12 inches of snow fell along the eastern slopes of the Nulato Hills.  Based on the observations of near to above freezing temperatures from the Marshall and Russian Mission AWSS's, it is likely that the snow changed to freezing rain in spots, and the freezing rain likely accumulated in excess of a tenth of an inch in spots. ||Zone 216: Based on the NWS observation of 8.7 inches of snow at McGrath, it is estimated that 8 to 12 inches of snow fell along the eastern slopes of the Nulato Hills.  Based on the observations of above freezing temperatures at the Huslia AWOS, the Kaltag ASOS, the Galena AWSS and the Ruby AWSS, it is likely that the snow changed to freezing rain in spots, and the freezing rain likely accumulated in excess of a tenth of an inch in spots. ||Zone 217: Based on the visibility reductions on the observations from Ambler AWOS there was likely 6 inches or more of snow at Ambler, and due to upslope flow, up to 1 foot of snow likely occurred on the south-facing slopes of the Brooks Range. ||Zone 218: The State of Alaska DOT Camp at Chandalar reported 7 inches of new snow in the 24 hours ending 600 AKST on the 15th, and 8 additional inches at 0600 AKST on the 16th, for a storm total of 15 inches. From DOT reports it is likely there were areas of blowing and drifting snow with near zero visibilities.  ||Zone 219: 9.7 inches of snow was observed at Bettles. From the DOT reports it appears likely that there some blowing and drifting snow and areas of freezing rain across the higher terrain, including parts of the Dalton Highway.||Zone 225: Denali Park Co-operative Observer reported a 24-hour rainfall of 0.27 inches of rain at 0800 AKST on the 15th, then 0.02 inches at 0800 AKST on the 16th, and 0.40 inches of rain at 0800 AKST on the 17th. Estimate that rain fell mostly on the morning of the 14th by 0800 AKST, and on the 16th after 0800 AKST. A long-time Park employee said the roads were sheets of ice and that State of Alaska DOT sanding trucks were slipping backwards and sideways. ||Zone 226: There were numerous reports from the DOT of ice along the Richardson Highway from MP 202 through MP 232. A weather spotter at Isabel Pass reported 8 inches of snowfall and ice accumulation from freezing rain. The freezing rain likely accumulated in excess of a tenth of an inch.

An intense hurricane force storm moved across the Aleutians into the eastern Bering Sea February 24th and 25th. This storm produced hurricane force wind as it moved through the region. This storm produced blizzard conditions along the Bering Sea coast from Bristol Bay north across the Kuskokwim Delta. Wind gusts were reported in excess of 100 mph in the Pribilof Islands and in Bristol Bay. Extensive damage occurred to many homes and buildings. This storm also produced a storm surge in the Bristol Bay region near Dillingham, Clarks Point and Ekuk.

A 962 mb low tracked north across the eastern Bering Sea on October 10-11, 2009, and into the Gulf of Anadyr on the evening of the 11th.  The storm brought a storm surge along the southern coast of the Seward Peninsula on the morning of the 11th, and tides were approximately 5 1/2 feet above mean sea level at Nome.  The storm surge produced flooding along the Nome-Council Road around the time of high tide on the morning of the 11th.
A strong area of low pressure in the eastern Bering Sea produced a period of high winds at Marshall on the morning of October 10th.  A wind gust of 60 mph was observed at the Marshall AWSS.

The combination of a very cold airmass that was established in late December, and a period of strong wind, combined to produce low wind chills in the passes of the Alaska Range and in the Upper Yukon Valley on January 8th and 9th. ||Here are some of the lowest wind chills that were observed:||Zone 224: Eagle ASOS: 62 below|Zone 225: Antler Creek RWIS (Parks Highway MP 244): 66 below|Zone 226: RWIS Site on the Tok Cutoff (Mentasta Pass MP 79.2): 67 below.

A 992 mb low in the Northeast Gulf of Alaska produced a period of heavy snow in portions of the Upper Tanana Valley south of the Alaska Highway.  Snow developed around 4:30 pm on the 12th south of the Robertson River Bridge off the Alaska Highway, and fell heavily at times during the evening hours.  The cooperative weather observer south of the Robertson River Bridge measured 6 inches of snow by 9:45 pm.  The snow tapered off during the early morning hours on the 13th, with a storm total of 8 inches.

A strong Chinook developed as a ridge of high pressure developed across the eastern Pacific and a trough of low pressure developed across eastern Russia.  This produced a period of high winds in the Alaska Range as well as parts of the Deltana Flats and the Upper Tanana Valley.  The most significant event occurred in Delta Junction (zone 223) about a mile from the Delta River. The high winds that were estimated between 80 and 95 mph blew down trees and as well as the roof of an occupied cabin. The south side of the roof was lifted off the cabin and ripped apart, landing in a pile more than 100 feet from the cabin. The other half of the roof lifted off the cabin and flipped, landing as a complete unit about 30 feet from the cabin. When the roof was blown off the cabin the wood stove in the cabin was moved about 2 feet and the interior stove pipe was lying on the floor several feet away. Estimate damage to this residence is $15,000. The high winds knocked out power and phone service in the area, which was restored to most homes later in the day. At one point during the morning, much of the city of Delta Junction was without power. At Whitestone Farms near Delta Junction, many trees were reported blown down. Minor damage was sustained to a building and a vehicle when a tree fell onto both, estimated damage $1700, a window was broken when a tree branch came through it, estimated damage $300, and metal roofing came off a portion of a building, estimate damage $1000. In Denali National Park and Preserve (zone 225), a number of trees were reported blown down.||The highest wind gusts that were measured or observed were:||Zone 220: Eagle Summit Meteorburst: Measured 68 mph/59 kt.|Zone 223: Delta Junction: Estimated 80 to 95 mph. The Whitestone Farms Davis instruments anemometer showed a winds speed just over 78 knots/90 mph before it stopped reporting. |Zone 224: NWS Spotter near Robertson River on Alaska Highway: Estimated 60 mph/52kt. |Zone 225: Antler Creek DOT RWIS (on the Parks Highway MP 244) measured 74 mph/64 kt.|Zone 226: Texas Condo: Measured 80 mph/69 kt.|          Washington Range: Measured 77 mph/67 kt.|          OP 12A: Measured 84 mph/73 kt.||The observations in zone 226 are all part of the Fort Greely mesonet.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low developed off the Queen Charlotte Islands down to 972 MB on Thursday afternoon 12/17 and moved rapidly northward under a southerly jet stream, making landfall around Icy Cape on the morning of 12/18. A strong front moved onto the SE Alaska coast on the morning of 12/18 causing wind gusts to 70 MPH for Prince of Wales Island. This same system brought heavy snow to the Haines area during the early morning hours of 12/18 due to warm air overrunning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg AWOS had a measured gust of 58 KT at 0136 AKST on 12/18. Gusts around 70 MPH were common near headlands on Prince of Wales Island. Peak winds exceeded 100 KT at Lincoln Rock (marine observation ) on the morning of 12/18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low developed off the Queen Charlotte Islands down to 972 MB on Thursday afternoon 12/17 and moved rapidly northward under a southerly jet stream, making landfall around Icy Cape on the morning of 12/18. A strong front moved onto the SE Alaska coast on the morning of 12/18 causing wind gusts to 70 MPH for Prince of Wales Island. This same system brought heavy snow to the Haines area during the early morning hours of 12/18 due to warm air overrunning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow continued into the morning of 12/18 due to warm overrunning. The heavy snow was focused up the Chilkat Highway on the night of 12/17. Haines Customs measured 11.0 inches of new snow for 24 hours ending at 0700 AKST on 12/18. Frequent winds to 40 MPH were observed at the Haines Airport during this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system was over the entire Gulf of Alaska on the night of 12/15. Low level cold air aided by a north wind persisted over northern SE Alaska into 12/18.  The arctic front that oscillated north and south over the Northern Panhandle on 12/18. The upper level flow was out of the south which brought moist air over the area and caused heavy snow for the Haines and Petersburg areas on 12/17 and 12/18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on the observations at Haines Airport, snow began for the area in the early morning hours of 12/16. Heavy accumulations did not occur until the early morning hours of the 17th. Haines Customs measured 13.0 inches of new snowfall at 0700 AKST on 12/17 and 14.0 inches new at 0700 on 12/18. A storm total of around 16 inches was estimated for downtown Haines on the 12/17 by a spotter, but the snow rapidly turned to rain near sea level on the morning of 12/17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1036 MB arctic High pressure system was over the Yukon on 12/22 with cold air in gap flow for northern SE Alaska. Warm moist air began to overrun this cold outflow resulting in heavy snow for several areas in the Northern Panhandle. Gap winds increased during the night of 12/22 as the high pressure cell began to rebuild. A 982 MB gale force low then moved into the central Gulf on the morning of 12/23 to further increase the pressure gradients and move in warm moist air at mid levels throughout the day for more snow and wind on 12/24.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters measured 4 to 6 inches inches of new snowfall in 12 hours ending around 1800 AKST on 12/23. Gale force winds occurred in Icy Strait just to the north of Hoonah, with some wind inland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1036 MB arctic High pressure system was over the Yukon on 12/22 with cold air in gap flow for northern SE Alaska. Gap winds increased during the night of 12/22 as the high pressure cell began to rebuild. A 982 MB gale force low then moved into the central Gulf on the morning of 12/23 to further increase the pressure gradients and move in warm moist air at mid levels throughout the day for more snow and wind on 12/24.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glacier Bay area had quite a mix of winter weather on 12/23. 4.6 inches of new snow was measured at 1330 AKST on 12/23 which was followed by rain and freezing rain. Outlying areas probably received higher amounts of new snow on 12/23. Some wind was reported at Bartlett Cove which implied that the more windy areas probably had wind gusts to 30 MPH as also inferred from NCEP surface analyses.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in downtown Haines measured a storm total of 5.3 inches of new snow at 1800 AKST on 12/23. 6-8 inches new additional snow fell on the night of 12/23. 8 more inches of snow fell during the day of 11/24. One spotter had a storm total of 16 inches that had compacted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thompson Pass RWIS reported 80 mph gust.

The arctic front that oscillated north and south over the Northern Panhandle on 12/18. The upper level flow was out of the south which brought moist air over the area and caused heavy snow for the Haines and Petersburg areas on 12/17 and 12/18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell at both Petersburg and Wrangell from the afternoon of 12/15 to the morning of 12/16. The Wrangell COOP observer measured 6.0 inches of new snowfall during the evening of 12/15. Storm totals ranged from 4 to 8 inches for this short lived event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another huge storm developed well south of the Alaska Peninsula on 12/24 on the heels of a Gulf Low. The associated front moved slowly onto the coast on 12/25 bringing heavy snow to the Haines area. later on the night of 12/24 through the morning of 12/25. The snow mixed with rain and freezing rain later on 12/25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts around Haines were highly variable. The deepest new snowfall was downtown Haines where a spotter measured 8.7 inches of new snow for 24 hours ending at 0800 AKST on 12/25. When compared to the Haines Airport ASOS, the heavy snow was during a period of 6 hours in the early morning. The snow started to turn to rain around 1100 AKST on 12/25 for a real, slushy mess.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 976 mb low near the northern Kamchatka Peninsula on the afternoon of the 26th tracked across the Chukotsk Peninsula during the afternoon of the 27th, and into the southern Chukchi Sea on the evening the 27th as a 983 mb low.  The low then tracked northeast across the Arctic Ocean on the 28th.  The storm brought blizzard conditions to much of the west coast as well as the arctic coasts of Alaska.||Zone 201: Blizzard conditions were observed from the early morning hours on the 28th through midnight.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less at Point Lay in snow and blowing snow.  The Point Lay AWOS had a peak wind gust of 54 mph/47kt. similar conditions were also observed at Atqasuk and Wainwright. The Atqasuk AWSS had a peak wind gust to 53 mph/46kt, and the Wainwright ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 52 mph/45kt. ||Zone 202: Blizzard conditions were observed at Barrow during the afternoon and evening hours on the 28th.  The blizzard conditions continued into the morning of the 1st.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter of a mile and the Barrow ASOS had a peak wind gust of 48 mph/42kt. ||Zone 203: Blizzard conditions were observed at Nuiqsut during the morning and early afternoon hours on the 26th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile in heavy snow and blowing snow.  The Nuiqsut ASOS reported sustained winds of around 25 mph with gusts to 37 mph/32kt. ||Zone 204: Blizzard conditions were observed at Barter Island during the early morning hours on the 26th. The visibility was reduced to zero at times in heavy blowing snow at Barter Island during the early morning hours of the 26th.  The Barter Island AWOS had sustained winds as high as 61 mph/53kt with gusts as high as 68 mph/59kt.||Zone 205: Blizzard conditions likely occurred from about noon on the 27th through midnight on the 28th.  This is based on conditions observed in passes of the northeastern Brooks Range (Anaktuvuk Pass) as well as the Kelly RAWS (KELA2) in zone 208.  The Ikpikpuk automated stream gage (IKPA2) in zone 205 also reported sustained winds of up to 43 mph/37kt.||Zone 206: Based on State of Alaska Department of Transportation (DOT) reports, blizzard conditions likely occurred at Atigun Pass on the 26th. The winds were reported as being in excess of 40 mph, and the visibility was reported as being near zero at times in blowing and drifting snow. The blizzard conditions re-developed again early on the morning of the 28th and continued until late afternoon. The DOT reported winds of 20 to 40 mph at Atigun pass with near zero visibility in blowing and drifting snow. ||Zone 207: Blizzard conditions were observed along the Chukchi Sea coast from the morning hours on the 27th through the afternoon hours on the 28th.  At Point Hope, the visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow. At Shishmaref, the visibility was reduced to zero at times in snow and blowing snow. The Shishmaref AWOS reported wind gusts to 44 mph/38kt.  At Kivalina, the visibility was reduced to zero at times in heavy snow and blowing snow.  The Kivalina ASOS reported wind gusts to 49 mph/43kt.||Zone 208: Blizzard conditions were observed during the afternoon and evening hours of the 27th at Noatak. The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  A peak wind gust of 48 mph/42kt was reported by the Noatak AWOS. ||Zone 209: Blizzard conditions were observed at Kotzebue during the morning hours of the 26th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to near zero in heavy snow and blowing snow.  The Kotzebue ASOS reported sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts as high as 53 mph/46kt.  The blizzard conditions re-developed again on the morning of the 27th, and continued into the late evening hours of the 27th.  The wind at the Kotzebue ASOS gusted as high as 64 mph/56kt, and the visibility was reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow. There was a third round of blizzard conditions during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 28th.  The visibility was reduced to near zero at Kotzebue, and the wind gusted to 46 mph/40kt. In Selawik, the visibility was reduced to one quarter of a mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  The Selawik AWOS reported wind gusts as high as 45 mph/39kt. ||Zone 210: Blizzard conditions were observed at Buckland during the morning and early afternoon hours on the 26th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to near zero in snow and blowing snow.  The Buckland AWOS reported sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph with wind gusts as high as 59 mph/51kt.  The blizzard conditions at Buckland re-developed briefly during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 27th. The visibility was again reduced to near zero, and the wind gusted as high as 54 mph/47kt.  ||Zone 211: Blizzard conditions were observed at Nome during the morning and afternoon hours on the 27th.  The visibility was reduced to near zero at times in heavy snow and blowing snow.  The Nome ASOS reported wind gusts to 45 mph/39kt. ||Zone 212: Blizzard conditions were observed at Saint Michael during the early morning hours on the 26th.  A northwest wind of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 48 mph/42kt was observed at the St. Michael AWSS.  The visibility was reduced to one quarter or a mile in snow and blowing snow. The wind gusted as high as 52 mph/45kt at the Koyuk AWOS during this time and the visibility was occasionally reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow. The blizzard conditions re-developed late on the morning of the 27th and continued into the early evening hours at Unalakleet.  The visibility was reduced to near zero at times.  The Unalakleet AWOS reported an east wind that gusted as high as 53 mph/46kt. ||Zone 213: Blizzard conditions as well as high winds were observed on Saint Lawrence Island during the morning hours of the 26th and along the Bering Strait coast during the morning and afternoon hours of the 26th.  The worst conditions were observed at Savoonga. Shortly after 1 am, the wind increased to sustained speeds of 75 to 85 mph.  Wind gusts as high as 99 mph were observed at the Savoonga AWOS.  There were unconfirmed reports of wind gusts as high as 116 mph about 500 yards south of the airport on the hills at the Savoonga wind farm.  The high winds lasted until around 6 am at which time the wind backed into the east to southeast and diminished.  At Gambell, the wind gusted as high as 61 mph, and was much more in line with what the pressure gradient would suggest. The high winds produced significant damage in the village on Savoonga.  At least three homes had significant damage with their roofs either partially or totally blown off.  Many homes had significant damage to their roofs and siding.  Several chimneys were blown off of houses and the chimney from the new school that was being built was found on the shore fast ice.  Gravel from the airport was blown one half mile into the village and damaged homes in the village.  Numerous phone and power lines were knocked down by the high winds, and fuel tanks along the side of many homes were blown over.  No injuries or fatalities were reported from this storm.|On the Bering Strait coast, the wind gusted as high as 86 mph/75kt at the Wales AWSS with blizzard conditions in heavy snow and blowing and drifting snow.  At the Teller AWSS, the wind gusted as high as 66 mph/57kt with blizzard conditions in heavy snow and blowing and drifting snow.||Zone 214: Blizzard conditions were observed across the Yukon Delta during the morning hours of the 27th. The visibility was reduced to near zero at times at Scammon Bay.  The Scammon Bay AWSS reported wind gusts as high as 61mph/53kt. The blizzard conditions also likely occurred inland, and the visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less at times at Mountain Village and Saint Marys, but there was a significant amount of missing data at the Mountain Village AWSS and the Saint Marys AWOS. ||Zone 215: Blizzard conditions were observed at Marshall during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 27th.  The visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  The wind gusted to 45 mph/39kt at the Marshall AWSS.||Zone 217: It is estimated that around 10 inches of snow fell in parts of the Upper Kobuk and Noatak Valleys, based on the heavy snow that fell in zone 209 at Kotzebue.  In addition, there was likely a significant amount of blowing and drifting snow.  The visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less at Ambler during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 28th, and the Ambler AWOS reported sustained wind of 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 39 mph/34kt.

CZ_NAME: CAPE FAIRWEATHER TO CAPE SUCKLING COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep storm moved into the Eastern Aleutians on the afternoon of 11/30. Warm, moist air with the associated front moved over the Eastern Gulf Coast which had cold air at lower levels. The result was heavy snow for Yakutat during the night of 11/30 into the early morning hours of 12/1. Yakutat got nearly six inches of new snow in a few hours around midnight. Snow rapidly changed to rain around 4 AM AKST on 12/1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSO Yakutat measured 5.8 inches of new snow at 2353 on the night of 11/30. An additional 0.3 inches fell before 0400 on 12/1 for a storm total of over 6 inches for a few hours around midnight. The snow changed rapidly to rain around 0400 on 12/1 and melted away very quickly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 970 mb low in the central Bering Sea on the afternoon of the 17th tracked to the southern Chukchi Sea on the afternoon of the 18th, and then along the arctic coast on the 19th.  This storm system brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to much of northern Alaska.  High winds were also observed in the passes of the Alaska Range. ||Zone 207: Blizzard conditions were observed at Kivalina and Point Hope on the 18th.  At the Kivalina ASOS the visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in heavy snow and blowing snow from late in the morning through late in the afternoon. An east to southeast wind of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 40 mph was observed.  Similar conditions were observed at the Point Hope AWOS during the afternoon hours. ||Zone 209: Blizzard conditions were observed at the Kotzebue Weather Service Office during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter of a mile or less in heavy snow and blowing snow.  An east wind of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to near 50 mph was observed during this event at the Kotzebue ASOS. The bodies of two men were found approximately 10 miles south of Kotzebue on the 18th.  They were reported missing on the 13th, and likely died due to hypothermia. ||Zone 210: Blizzard conditions were observed at Buckland during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 18th.  The visibility at the Buckland AWOS was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  An east wind of 25 to 40 mph with gusts to near 50 mph was observed during this event. ||Zone 211: Blizzard conditions were observed at times at the Nome WSO during the morning and early afternoon hours on the 18th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow.  An east wind of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 40 mph was observed at the Nome ASOS. ||Zone 212: Heavy snow likely occurred along the eastern Norton Sound and across the Nulato Hills. Thirteen inches of snow was observed at the Kaltag Coop in the Nulato Hills (zone 216), and it is likely that similar amounts were observed in the Nulato hills in zone 212.  Seven inches or more of snow also likely fell along the eastern Norton Sound. Blizzard conditions were observed at Unalakleet during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th.  The visibility at the Unalakleet AWOS was frequently less than one quarter of a mile in snow and blowing snow.  An east wind of 30 to 45 mph with gusts to 50 mph was observed at the Unalakleet AWOS during this event. ||Zone 213: Blizzard conditions were observed at Gambell during the late evening of the 17th into the morning hours of the 18th.  The visibility at the Gambell AWOS was frequently reduced to one quarter of a mile or less.  An east wind of 35 to 50 mph with gusts as high as 59 mph were observed during this event. Blizzard conditions were also briefly observed at Savoonga during the early morning hours on the 18th. ||Zone 214: Blizzard conditions were observed along the Yukon Delta coast during the early morning hours on the 18th. The wind gusted as high as 81 mph at the Cape Romanzof AWOS and the visibility was reduced to zero at times in snow and blowing snow. The visibility was also reduced to one quarter mile or less in heavy snow and blowing snow at Scammon Bay with gusts to 40 to 45 mph at the Scammon Bay AWSS.  Inland, heavy snow was observed at Saint Marys and Mountain Village, however, the wind was not as strong as along the coast, and was generally in the 20 to 35 mph range. ||Zone 215: Based on the observation of 13 inches of snow at the Kaltag Coop (zone 216), it is likely that 8 inches of snow fell in parts of lower Yukon Valley. ||Zone 216: Heavy snow was observed at the Kaltag coop.  Thirteen inches of snow was observed during this event at Kaltag. ||Zone 217: Heavy snow and significant drifting snow was observed at Ambler during the morning hours on the 18th through the early morning hours on the 19th.  There were public reports of up to 36 inches of snow at Ambler, with snow drifts of 15 to 20 feet.  The visibility at the Ambler AWOS was frequently less than one mile, and occasionally as low as one quarter of a mile during this event.||Zone 218: Heavy snow was observed at the Wiseman Co-operative observer from late on the 18th through the morning of the 19th.  A total of 16 inches of snow was observed from midnight until 1 pm at Wiseman during this event. ||Zone 219: Heavy snow was observed at Bettles from the afternoon hours on the 18th through the morning hours on the 19th.  A total of 16.3 inches of snow was observed at Bettles during this event. ||Zone 220: Snow and strong winds were observed at Eagle Summit along the Steese Highway during the morning hours on the 19th.  It was estimated from DOT reports that the wind was gusting over 45 mph with low visibilities with blowing and heavy drifting snow.||Zone 221: Heavy snow was observed at Tanana from the afternoon hours on the 18th through the morning hours on the 19th.  A weather spotter in Tanana observed 12 inches of snow from this event. ||Zone 225: Heavy snow was observed along the Parks Highway.  The DOT reported 12 inches of snow at Milepost 215.8.  In addition to the heavy snowfall, strong winds were observed in the passes, with a wind gust to 63kt/73 mph at the Antler Creek DOT site at milepost 244. ||Zone 226: Up to 8 inches of snow was observed by a weather spotter from this storm.  A wind gust to 63kt/73 mph was observed at Texas Condo, which is part of the Fort Greely mesonet.

A 970 mb low in the central Bering Sea on the afternoon of the 17th tracked to the southern Chukchi Sea on the afternoon of the 18th, and then along the arctic coast on the 19th.  This storm system brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to much of northern Alaska.  High winds were also observed in the passes of the Alaska Range. ||Zone 207: Blizzard conditions were observed at Kivalina and Point Hope on the 18th.  At the Kivalina ASOS the visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in heavy snow and blowing snow from late in the morning through late in the afternoon. An east to southeast wind of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 40 mph was observed.  Similar conditions were observed at the Point Hope AWOS during the afternoon hours. ||Zone 209: Blizzard conditions were observed at the Kotzebue Weather Service Office during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter of a mile or less in heavy snow and blowing snow.  An east wind of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to near 50 mph was observed during this event at the Kotzebue ASOS. The bodies of two men were found approximately 10 miles south of Kotzebue on the 18th.  They were reported missing on the 13th, and likely died due to hypothermia. ||Zone 210: Blizzard conditions were observed at Buckland during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 18th.  The visibility at the Buckland AWOS was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  An east wind of 25 to 40 mph with gusts to near 50 mph was observed during this event. ||Zone 211: Blizzard conditions were observed at times at the Nome WSO during the morning and early afternoon hours on the 18th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow.  An east wind of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 40 mph was observed at the Nome ASOS. ||Zone 212: Heavy snow likely occurred along the eastern Norton Sound and across the Nulato Hills. Thirteen inches of snow was observed at the Kaltag Coop in the Nulato Hills (zone 216), and it is likely that similar amounts were observed in the Nulato hills in zone 212.  Seven inches or more of snow also likely fell along the eastern Norton Sound. Blizzard conditions were observed at Unalakleet during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th.  The visibility at the Unalakleet AWOS was frequently less than one quarter of a mile in snow and blowing snow.  An east wind of 30 to 45 mph with gusts to 50 mph was observed at the Unalakleet AWOS during this event. ||Zone 213: Blizzard conditions were observed at Gambell during the late evening of the 17th into the morning hours of the 18th.  The visibility at the Gambell AWOS was frequently reduced to one quarter of a mile or less.  An east wind of 35 to 50 mph with gusts as high as 59 mph were observed during this event. Blizzard conditions were also briefly observed at Savoonga during the early morning hours on the 18th. ||Zone 214: Blizzard conditions were observed along the Yukon Delta coast during the early morning hours on the 18th. The wind gusted as high as 81 mph at the Cape Romanzof AWOS and the visibility was reduced to zero at times in snow and blowing snow. The visibility was also reduced to one quarter mile or less in heavy snow and blowing snow at Scammon Bay with gusts to 40 to 45 mph at the Scammon Bay AWSS.  Inland, heavy snow was observed at Saint Marys and Mountain Village, however, the wind was not as strong as along the coast, and was generally in the 20 to 35 mph range. ||Zone 215: Based on the observation of 13 inches of snow at the Kaltag Coop (zone 216), it is likely that 8 inches of snow fell in parts of lower Yukon Valley. ||Zone 216: Heavy snow was observed at the Kaltag coop.  Thirteen inches of snow was observed during this event at Kaltag. ||Zone 217: Heavy snow and significant drifting snow was observed at Ambler during the morning hours on the 18th through the early morning hours on the 19th.  There were public reports of up to 36 inches of snow at Ambler, with snow drifts of 15 to 20 feet.  The visibility at the Ambler AWOS was frequently less than one mile, and occasionally as low as one quarter of a mile during this event.||Zone 218: Heavy snow was observed at the Wiseman Co-operative observer from late on the 18th through the morning of the 19th.  A total of 16 inches of snow was observed from midnight until 1 pm at Wiseman during this event. ||Zone 219: Heavy snow was observed at Bettles from the afternoon hours on the 18th through the morning hours on the 19th.  A total of 16.3 inches of snow was observed at Bettles during this event. ||Zone 220: Snow and strong winds were observed at Eagle Summit along the Steese Highway during the morning hours on the 19th.  It was estimated from DOT reports that the wind was gusting over 45 mph with low visibilities with blowing and heavy drifting snow.||Zone 221: Heavy snow was observed at Tanana from the afternoon hours on the 18th through the morning hours on the 19th.  A weather spotter in Tanana observed 12 inches of snow from this event. ||Zone 225: Heavy snow was observed along the Parks Highway.  The DOT reported 12 inches of snow at Milepost 215.8.  In addition to the heavy snowfall, strong winds were observed in the passes, with a wind gust to 63kt/73 mph at the Antler Creek DOT site at milepost 244. ||Zone 226: Up to 8 inches of snow was observed by a weather spotter from this storm.  A wind gust to 63kt/73 mph was observed at Texas Condo, which is part of the Fort Greely mesonet.

A 976 mb low near the northern Kamchatka Peninsula on the afternoon of the 26th tracked across the Chukotsk Peninsula during the afternoon of the 27th, and into the southern Chukchi Sea on the evening the 27th as a 983 mb low.  The low then tracked northeast across the Arctic Ocean on the 28th.  The storm brought blizzard conditions to much of the west coast as well as the arctic coasts of Alaska.||Zone 201: Blizzard conditions were observed from the early morning hours on the 28th through midnight.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less at Point Lay in snow and blowing snow.  The Point Lay AWOS had a peak wind gust of 54 mph/47kt. similar conditions were also observed at Atqasuk and Wainwright. The Atqasuk AWSS had a peak wind gust to 53 mph/46kt, and the Wainwright ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 52 mph/45kt. ||Zone 202: Blizzard conditions were observed at Barrow during the afternoon and evening hours on the 28th.  The blizzard conditions continued into the morning of the 1st.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter of a mile and the Barrow ASOS had a peak wind gust of 48 mph/42kt. ||Zone 203: Blizzard conditions were observed at Nuiqsut during the morning and early afternoon hours on the 26th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile in heavy snow and blowing snow.  The Nuiqsut ASOS reported sustained winds of around 25 mph with gusts to 37 mph/32kt. ||Zone 204: Blizzard conditions were observed at Barter Island during the early morning hours on the 26th. The visibility was reduced to zero at times in heavy blowing snow at Barter Island during the early morning hours of the 26th.  The Barter Island AWOS had sustained winds as high as 61 mph/53kt with gusts as high as 68 mph/59kt.||Zone 205: Blizzard conditions likely occurred from about noon on the 27th through midnight on the 28th.  This is based on conditions observed in passes of the northeastern Brooks Range (Anaktuvuk Pass) as well as the Kelly RAWS (KELA2) in zone 208.  The Ikpikpuk automated stream gage (IKPA2) in zone 205 also reported sustained winds of up to 43 mph/37kt.||Zone 206: Based on State of Alaska Department of Transportation (DOT) reports, blizzard conditions likely occurred at Atigun Pass on the 26th. The winds were reported as being in excess of 40 mph, and the visibility was reported as being near zero at times in blowing and drifting snow. The blizzard conditions re-developed again early on the morning of the 28th and continued until late afternoon. The DOT reported winds of 20 to 40 mph at Atigun pass with near zero visibility in blowing and drifting snow. ||Zone 207: Blizzard conditions were observed along the Chukchi Sea coast from the morning hours on the 27th through the afternoon hours on the 28th.  At Point Hope, the visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow. At Shishmaref, the visibility was reduced to zero at times in snow and blowing snow. The Shishmaref AWOS reported wind gusts to 44 mph/38kt.  At Kivalina, the visibility was reduced to zero at times in heavy snow and blowing snow.  The Kivalina ASOS reported wind gusts to 49 mph/43kt.||Zone 208: Blizzard conditions were observed during the afternoon and evening hours of the 27th at Noatak. The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  A peak wind gust of 48 mph/42kt was reported by the Noatak AWOS. ||Zone 209: Blizzard conditions were observed at Kotzebue during the morning hours of the 26th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to near zero in heavy snow and blowing snow.  The Kotzebue ASOS reported sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts as high as 53 mph/46kt.  The blizzard conditions re-developed again on the morning of the 27th, and continued into the late evening hours of the 27th.  The wind at the Kotzebue ASOS gusted as high as 64 mph/56kt, and the visibility was reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow. There was a third round of blizzard conditions during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 28th.  The visibility was reduced to near zero at Kotzebue, and the wind gusted to 46 mph/40kt. In Selawik, the visibility was reduced to one quarter of a mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  The Selawik AWOS reported wind gusts as high as 45 mph/39kt. ||Zone 210: Blizzard conditions were observed at Buckland during the morning and early afternoon hours on the 26th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to near zero in snow and blowing snow.  The Buckland AWOS reported sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph with wind gusts as high as 59 mph/51kt.  The blizzard conditions at Buckland re-developed briefly during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 27th. The visibility was again reduced to near zero, and the wind gusted as high as 54 mph/47kt.  ||Zone 211: Blizzard conditions were observed at Nome during the morning and afternoon hours on the 27th.  The visibility was reduced to near zero at times in heavy snow and blowing snow.  The Nome ASOS reported wind gusts to 45 mph/39kt. ||Zone 212: Blizzard conditions were observed at Saint Michael during the early morning hours on the 26th.  A northwest wind of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 48 mph/42kt was observed at the St. Michael AWSS.  The visibility was reduced to one quarter or a mile in snow and blowing snow. The wind gusted as high as 52 mph/45kt at the Koyuk AWOS during this time and the visibility was occasionally reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow. The blizzard conditions re-developed late on the morning of the 27th and continued into the early evening hours at Unalakleet.  The visibility was reduced to near zero at times.  The Unalakleet AWOS reported an east wind that gusted as high as 53 mph/46kt. ||Zone 213: Blizzard conditions as well as high winds were observed on Saint Lawrence Island during the morning hours of the 26th and along the Bering Strait coast during the morning and afternoon hours of the 26th.  The worst conditions were observed at Savoonga. Shortly after 1 am, the wind increased to sustained speeds of 75 to 85 mph.  Wind gusts as high as 99 mph were observed at the Savoonga AWOS.  There were unconfirmed reports of wind gusts as high as 116 mph about 500 yards south of the airport on the hills at the Savoonga wind farm.  The high winds lasted until around 6 am at which time the wind backed into the east to southeast and diminished.  At Gambell, the wind gusted as high as 61 mph, and was much more in line with what the pressure gradient would suggest. The high winds produced significant damage in the village on Savoonga.  At least three homes had significant damage with their roofs either partially or totally blown off.  Many homes had significant damage to their roofs and siding.  Several chimneys were blown off of houses and the chimney from the new school that was being built was found on the shore fast ice.  Gravel from the airport was blown one half mile into the village and damaged homes in the village.  Numerous phone and power lines were knocked down by the high winds, and fuel tanks along the side of many homes were blown over.  No injuries or fatalities were reported from this storm.|On the Bering Strait coast, the wind gusted as high as 86 mph/75kt at the Wales AWSS with blizzard conditions in heavy snow and blowing and drifting snow.  At the Teller AWSS, the wind gusted as high as 66 mph/57kt with blizzard conditions in heavy snow and blowing and drifting snow.||Zone 214: Blizzard conditions were observed across the Yukon Delta during the morning hours of the 27th. The visibility was reduced to near zero at times at Scammon Bay.  The Scammon Bay AWSS reported wind gusts as high as 61mph/53kt. The blizzard conditions also likely occurred inland, and the visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less at times at Mountain Village and Saint Marys, but there was a significant amount of missing data at the Mountain Village AWSS and the Saint Marys AWOS. ||Zone 215: Blizzard conditions were observed at Marshall during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 27th.  The visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  The wind gusted to 45 mph/39kt at the Marshall AWSS.||Zone 217: It is estimated that around 10 inches of snow fell in parts of the Upper Kobuk and Noatak Valleys, based on the heavy snow that fell in zone 209 at Kotzebue.  In addition, there was likely a significant amount of blowing and drifting snow.  The visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less at Ambler during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 28th, and the Ambler AWOS reported sustained wind of 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 39 mph/34kt.
