CZ_NAME: EASTERN BARANOF ISLAND AND SOUTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic high was in place over northwest Canada and southeast Alaska. A weather system moved across the Gulf of Alaska and onshore the southeast panhandle...creating an overrunning snow event and  blizzard conditions over northern Lynn Canal region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A frontal band moved onshore over Southeast Alaska the night of 3 Jan, developing an overrunning snow event for the central and southern panhandle starting early on the 3rd into the following afternoon. Hidden Falls, a CO-OP station on eastern Baranof Island reported, for roughly a 60 hour period ending at 05/1700 they received 35.1 inches of snow.  The bulk of the snow fell from the evening of the 3rd to 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic high was in place over northwest Canada and southeast Alaska. A weather system moved across the Gulf of Alaska and onshore the southeast panhandle...creating an overrunning snow event and  blizzard conditions over northern Lynn Canal region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A frontal band moved onshore over Southeast the night of 3 Jan, developing an overrunning snow event for the central and southern panhandle into the following afternoon. The heavier snow started early in the evening on the 3rd and persisted into the following morning with slowly warming temperatures that changed the snow over to rain late in the morning hours. Annette Weather Service Office reported a snow total of 6 inches, with a spotter about 12 miles north of Ketchikan reported a total of 7 inches before it changed to rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass persisted across northwest Canada and southeast Alaska panhandle. An Arctic front on the 8th was positioned off shore of the panhandle.  A low moved across the gulf and into southeast Alaska on the 9th. A trailing low moved into southeast Alaska the 10th that continued with another overrunning snow event across the area as the arctic air in the panhandle was replaced with warmer air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gale force low moved across the gulf with moisture overrunning arctic air over the Southeast Alaska panhandle. Storm totals for the event in the area, ranged between 1 and 2 feet. The coop in Haines reported a daily snowfall of 16.8 inches the morning of the 9th and a storm total of 20.8 inches for the event. A spotter near Haines also reported 15 inches around 11 AM AKST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass persisted across northwest Canada and southeast Alaska panhandle. An Arctic front on the 8th was positioned off shore of the panhandle.  A low moved across the gulf and into southeast Alaska on the 9th. A trailing low moved into southeast Alaska the 10th that continued with another overrunning snow event across the area as the arctic air in the panhandle was replaced with warmer air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north winds (sustained 35 to 40 mph and gusts to 55 mph) combined with overrunning snow to create blizzard conditions for nearly 24 hours in Skagway. A report from the Canadian Highway Department of an avalanche closed White pass road overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low developed over the southwestern Gulf of Alaska and moved northeast across the panhandle in to northern British Columbia. Very strong southerly winds (60 to 80 mph gusts) were associated with the front as it moved over the southern half of the panhandle on the night of the 29th. One to three feet of snow fell over the northern Lynn Canal region with the low passage.  A strong band of trailing snow showers impacted the north central panhandle with up to a foot of snow as well into the night of the 30th.

An arctic high was in place over northwest Canada and southeast Alaska. A weather system moved across the Gulf of Alaska and onshore the southeast panhandle...creating an overrunning snow event and  blizzard conditions over northern Lynn Canal region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snowfall leading up to and through the event.  Strong sustained north winds to 25 mph with gusts of 35 to 50 mph...and locally stronger along Lynn Canal south of Haines, reduced visibilites to near zero for several periods during the afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass persisted across northwest Canada and southeast Alaska panhandle. An Arctic front on the 8th was positioned off shore of the panhandle.  A low moved across the gulf and into southeast Alaska on the 9th. A trailing low moved into southeast Alaska the 10th that continued with another overrunning snow event across the area as the arctic air in the panhandle was replaced with warmer air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gale force low moved across the gulf with moisture overrunning arctic air over the Southeast Alaska panhandle. A spotter in Hyder reported 18 inches of snow in 24 hours.  Hidden Inlet received 12.7 inches by 8 AM AKST the 9th and a storm total of 15.5 inches.

Cold arctic air was entrenched across the inner channels of Southeast Alaska as the system was approaching, leading to a significant snowfall event for the southern panhandle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 988 mb low approximatly 500 miles south-southwest of Saint Lawrence Island at 4 pm on the 5th slowly tracked to the northeast and was about 250 miles southwest by 4 am on the 7th.  The low brought blizzard conditions to parts of Saint Lawrence Island from the late evening hours on the 5th through the early afternoon hours on the 6th.  The worst conditions were observed at Gambell where the visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.  The wind at the Gambell AWOS was out of the northeast at sustained speeds of 30 to 40 mph with a peak wind gust of 47 mph/41 kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure well off the Arctic Coast on the 14th gradually built into the Chukchi Sea by the 17th.  The ridge of high pressure produced an unseasonably cold air mass across much of northern Alaska.  During this time, a large complex of low pressure was in the Gulf of Alaska. Similar wind chills were likely observed in passes and across the higher elevations of the northwestern Brooks Range. ||Zone 206: Temperatures of 25 to 35 below zero combined with a northeast wind of 20 to 30 mph to produce wind chills as low as 70 below at the Anaktuvuk Pass AWOS from the early morning hours on the 15th through the early morning hours on the 18th. A series of low pressure centers developed over the north central Pacific Ocean and moved over the Gulf of Alaska and the panhandle through the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hoonah coop measured 8.1 inches of new snow by 9 am. Nearly all of the new snow fell in the 12 hour period leading up to 9 am AKST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold arctic air was in place over the northern half the panhandle and interior valleys near British Columbia. A frontal band moved across the gulf developing overrunning snow across the northern panhandle the 6th. Another trailing low developed and moved northward across the panhandle with another band of heavy snow on the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coop station at Skagway Power measured 7.0 inches new snow by 7 am on the 7th. Based on estimates of observations at the Skagway airport, a few miles away, the main snow accumulation was over by 3 am.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief overrunning snow event occurred as weak trough moved across the northern panhandle. The heavier snow amounts were limited to the northern portion primarily near the border. The coop at the Haines Customs measured a new snow total of 5.8 inches primarily within 12 hours of 3 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold arctic air was in place over the northern half the panhandle and interior valleys near British Columbia. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moisture was lifted over the cold arctic air over the northern panhandle resulting in a good overrunning event of moderate to heavy snow.  The coop in Haines reported 13 inches of snow since 8 am at 4 pm.  An event total of 17 inches by 930 pm was reported by the coop.  A spotter 6 miles south of Haines reported an event total of 15 inches of snow. The spotter reported that at least 2 trees were toppled due the weight of the snow.

It was reported that 8 feet of road was falling into the river each hour during the afternoon hours on the 7th. The water gradually receded by the afternoon of the 8th. The damage estimates for this event include the costs to repair the levee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a strong cold front that crossed the area on the 26th there were periods of heavy snowfall from approximately 9 am until 8 pm on the 27th.  It is estimated that at least 6 inches of snow fell at Atigun Pass.

An ice jam below the village of Chalkyitsik caused water to back up in the Black river and caused minor flooding in the village on May 6th.  There were a total of 10 homes that had minor flooding, and many yards were submerged by the flood waters.

An ice jam developed on the Chatanika River at the Elliot Highway Bridge during the evening of April 30th. The ice jam caused water to be diverted into the surrounding woods and low lying areas adjacent to the river. Water was reported to be over the old railroad trail and flooded the campground in spots. The ice jam released during the afternoon of May 1st. No property damages were reported. Event continued in May.

An ice jam flood occurred near MP 34 along the Steese Highway during the early morning hours on the 30th and continued into the afternoon hours.  Several homes were surrounded by water and driveways were flooded, but the Steese Highway was not impacted. The ice jam released during the afternoon and water levels fell below bankfull. No property damages were reported.
An above-average snowpack and extra warm temperatures in early May created overflow of water out of the Nome River covering the Kougarok Road at mile 14 on May 4th and 5th.

CZ_NAME: WRN TANANA VLY WRN YUKON VLY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice Jammed about 50 miles downstream of Tanana on the 11th and ice continued to pack into the back of the jam and stopped near the village of Tanana on the morning of the 12th. Flooding began in Tanana at approximately 4 am on the 12th. The water levels peaked at around 3 pm on the 12th, and it was the 2nd highest crest on record. Residents of the village reported that this was the worst flooding in 50 years, and the water levels were only 3 feet shy of the record flood of 1937. |Many sections of the village were flooded including the road to the airport. One house was washed away by the flood waters, and 30 homes had major flood damage. Approximately 80 people were evacuated from the village.  By 5 pm on the 12th the water levels began to fall, but remained above bankfull until the late evening hours on the 13th. The damage estimates for this event include the costs to repair and replace homes that were damaged and destroyed as well as costs to repair and replace public infrastructure and equipment.

A strong difference in pressure between a 993 mb low in the Northern Bering Sea, and a 1034 mb high in Northern Canada, produced a period of high winds on Saint Lawrence Island and along the Bering Strait Coast during the late evening hours on the 16th through the morning hours on the 17th.  Here are the highest wind gusts that were observed:||Wales (AWSS): 74 mph/64kt|Savoonga (AWSO): 66 mph/57kt|Teller (AWSS) 60 mph/52kt.

A large surge of water and ice moving down the Yukon River produced minor flooding at Anvik. Water levels started to rise at about 8:30 pm on the 16th, and water was covering Front Street in the village. Two homes had basement flooding. The water levels remained high through the 18th, and then began to recede on 19th. No information available on property damage, an estimate is guessed at $2000.

A significant run of water and ice along with an ice jam that extended 10 miles up river of Marshall produced moderate flooding in the village of Marshall. By the morning of the 19th, water was getting into the low parts of the village and the road to the airport was beginning to flood. The water continued to rise, and by the evening of the 20th about one quarter mile of the road to the airport was under water. As the water continued to rise, low lying areas of the village were flooded by the morning of the 21st. The water levels began to slowly fall on the 22nd, however, low lying areas around the village remained under water and the road to the airport remained impassable. The water slowly receded on the 23rd, and by the evening of the 24th the water levels dropped in the lower part of the village with dry ground almost to the bank.  The road to the airport continued to have up to 2 feet of water over a section that is about 100 yards across. No information available on property damage, a preliminary estimate is guessed at $40,000.

The combination of a significant amount of water and ice flowing down the Yukon river along with ice jams near Kotlik and shore-fast ice at the mouth of the Yukon River produced minor flood in Kotlik during the afternoon hours of the 25th through the early morning hours of the 26th.  Water came over some roads on the north side of the village near the new airport, but the roads were still passable and no homes were flooded. No information available on property damage, a preliminary estimate is guessed at $0.

A significant amount of water and ice combined with ice jams in the vicinity of the village as well as downriver produced flooding in the village of Alakanuk. The flooding was compounded by 2 to 5 miles of shore-fast ice at the mouth of the Yukon River. The water levels rose during the early morning hours on the 23rd, and started to flood parts of the village. By the afternoon of the 23rd, water inundated many of the low lying structures. The water levels continued to rise, and by the 24th there were several homes along the river that were flooded with 1 to 3 feet of water. The water levels began to fall later in the evening, and were back within bank full by the late evening hours of the 25th or the early morning hours on the 26th. No information available on property damage, a preliminary estimate is guessed at $35,000.

A significant run of water and ice produced moderate flooding in the village of Russian Mission. By 5:30 pm on the 18th, the airport was surrounded by water and the road to the airport was becoming impassable. By the morning of the 19th, the runway at the airport as well as the road to the airport were underwater, and several homes were surrounded by water. The water levels continued to rise, and by the morning of the 20th, the airport was under approximately 18 inches of water, and 8 homes were flooded.  It was estimated that areas within approximately 400 yards of the river were flooded.    By the afternoon of the 22nd, the water levels were beginning to slowly fall, but some roads continued to remain inundated and the airport remained unusable. The water levels continued to slowly drop, and fell below bank full by the evening of the 23rd. No information available on property damage, a preliminary estimate is guessed at $20,000 consisting of $2000 per home and the remainder for road repair and other property.

A flood of historic proportions began in Eagle along the Yukon River around 2 am on the morning of the 4th.  In the week leading up to the flood, temperatures were unseasonably warm as a strong ridge of high pressure developed along the Alaska-Yukon border on April 27th and persisted until May 3rd.  In fact, record high temperatures were either tied or broken on the 4 days preceding the flood. The record warmth caused side streams to fill the river channel before the ice on the river broke apart. When it finally broke, it created large chunks of ice, some the size of small houses that jammed together and created dams of water as the ice jam shifted and broke apart during the next two days.  The ice was unusually thick in the Eagle area, and up to 7 feet in some locations along the river. By noon on the 4th, the old village of Eagle was flooded with water halfway up most houses, and some houses had water up to their roofs.  Parts of Eagle were flooded with water over Front Street.  By the evening of the 4th, the water rose an additional 4 to 5 feet and pushed ice over the retaining wall into Front Street and buildings. By the afternoon on the 5th, homes in the old village of Eagle were completely destroyed by ice, and in other areas in Eagle the water and ice was to the second story of homes. A business that was constructed to be more than 10 feet above the previous high water mark of 1937 was less than one foot above the water. At around midnight, the water came up further and ice smashed all structures along Front Street, and some buildings were lifted off their foundations and either moved or floated down river and were destroyed. On the night of the 6th, the water levels dropped about 30 feet and the worst of the flooding was over. In total, 26 homes were destroyed, and there were 7 homes with major damage. ||There were no serious injuries or deaths reported as a result of the flooding, but by the time the flooding was over it caused the worst flooding on record to the community of Eagle. The damage estimates for this event include the costs to repair and replace homes that were damaged and destroyed as well as costs to repair and replace public infrastructure and equipment.

An ice jam downriver of Kobuk led to minor flooding when the water went over the bank in the lower part of town.  The water did not make it over the road, and did not cause flooding to any structures in town. Estimated property damage is $0.

A large surge of water and ice moving down the Yukon River produced minor flooding at Grayling. During the early morning hours of the 17th, the water levels rose significantly and inundated the road to the airport making it accessible only by boat. The water levels dropped during the early morning hours of the 19th. No homes were flooded in the village of Grayling. Estimated property damage $0.

A large surge of water and ice moving down the Yukon River produced minor flooding in Nulato. There was approximately 2 feet of water over the road to the village water supply well, and there was about 8 inches of water over the road to the power plant at approximately 9 am on the 15th.  The flooding was minor and no homes were flooded. The water levels dropped below bank full on the morning of the 17th. Estimated property damage is $0.

An ice jam developed on the Chatanika River at the Elliot Highway Bridge during the evening of April 30th. The ice jam caused water to be diverted into the surrounding woods and low lying areas adjacent to the river. Water was reported to be over the old railroad trail and flooded the campground in spots. The ice jam released during the afternoon of May 1st. Estimated damage amount is $0.00.

The breakup front stopped just downstream of Stevens Village during the late morning hours on the 9th, and solid ice was in place where the river stops braiding and entering the canyon below Stevens Village.  Due to the extreme flooding that was experienced upriver in Eagle on May 4-6, 2009, nearly the entire village was evacuated by plane to Fairbanks prior to the onset of the flood. The water levels rose over 20 feet during the night of the 8th into the morning of the 9th. Water levels continued to rise rapidly during the afternoon of the 9th, and by approximately 5 pm the road along the river at Stevens Village began to flood. The water levels continued to rise during the evening and into the morning hours of the 10th. By the early morning hours on the 10th, there was widespread flooding in town with water up to the highest house in town. Only the school and approximately 5 houses in the village were spared by the flood waters, and most homes sustained major flood damage. In total, 3 homes were destroyed and 31 homes had major damage.  All power and phone service to the village was compromised by the flooding. Homes along the river received a significant amount of ice damage, and the ice knocked down all utility poles along the river front. The road that runs from the village to the airport sustained significant damage in several places and was impassable.  The water levels crested at approximately 5am on the 11th, and then began to recede, but the water did not go below bankfull until the afternoon or evening of the 12th. The damage estimates for this event include the costs to repair and replace homes that were damaged and destroyed as well as costs to repair and replace public infrastructure and equipment.

A large volume of water and a considerable amount of ice moved down river after the historic flooding at Eagle.  A 35 mile long ice run started to move by Circle during the evening of the 6th and jammed about 10 miles downstream of Circle. This caused water and ice to back into the channel in front of Circle.  The water crested during the early morning hours on the 7th, and then began to drop as the ice jam broke downriver.  This led to moderate flooding in Circle.  Chunks of ice came into town and damaged a few buildings along the riverbank. The store in town had about 20 inches of water at the highest point, but some homes closer to the Yukon River were flooded with water several feet deep. The damage estimates for this event include the costs to repair and replace homes that were damaged and destroyed as well as costs to repair and replace public infrastructure and equipment.

A large volume of water and a considerable amount of ice moved down river after the historic flooding at Eagle and moderate flooding at Circle. A 35 mile long ice run that moved by Circle during the evening of the 6th, diminished to a 20 mile long ice run at it passed Fort Yukon. The water levels rose rapidly at Fort Yukon during the late evening of the 7th, and several streets were flooded and people were evacuated from the low lying areas that were flooding. The flooding became more severe overnight as water began to inundate portions of the village with water several feet deep in some homes and low lying areas near the river. About one half of the village flooded and the main taxiway at the airport was damaged. The levee in front of town also received some damage. The water levels remained high and the flooding continued into the 8th. The water levels began to recede on the morning of the 9th, and dropped approximately 7 feet by the evening of the 9th. The damage estimates for this event include the costs to repair and replace homes that were damaged and destroyed as well as costs to repair and replace public infrastructure and equipment.

An ice jam developed in the Tanana River about one half mile downstream of the Boondox area during the morning of the 28th. The ice jam caused the Tanana River water to flow overland into Piledriver slough and across Portions of the old Richardson Highway where the water was 3 feet deep in spots and made the highway impassable. Private property and some residences were surrounded by water. The flooding was compounded by warm temperatures that caused considerable snowmelt and raised the water levels significantly in the Tanana River. Three to four homes had minor damage at or near the first floor level and a few property owners had minor damage to their access roads; thus a total of $500.00.

An intense north Pacific storm moved across the Alaska Peninsula October 9th and 10th. The storm produced high wind across Kodiak Island and the Bristol Bay area. Along with the Strong wind heavy rain fell across Kodiak Island and the southern Kenai Peninsula. The city of Kodiak measured 7.5 inches of rain on the 9th and 10th. The heavy rain resulted in minor small stream flooding and a few mud slides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chigmit Mountain reported gust to 104 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between a 1032 mb high well off the north coast of Alaska in the Arctic Ocean, and a 973 mb low near Kodiak Island, produced a period of high winds at Barter Island during the evening hours of the 21st into the morning hours of the 22nd.  The wind frequently gusted over 60 mph, with a peak wind gust of 68 mph at the Barter Island AWSS at 4 am AKDT.

A strong pressure gradient between a 1032 mb high well off the north coast of Alaska in the Arctic Ocean, and a 973 mb low near Kodiak Island, produced a period of strong winds along the Central Beaufort Sea Coast from late on the evening of the 21st through the afternoon hours of the 22nd.  The strong winds combined with falling snow to produce blizzard conditions.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less. The wind at the Deadhorse ASOS frequently gusted above 50 mph, with a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

A 972 mb low in the Northeast Gulf of Alaska Combined with a 1028 mb high near the Brooks Range to produce heavy upslope snow across the north slopes of the Eastern Alaska Range and in portions of the Upper Tanana Valley south of the Alaska Highway. Snow developed at approximately 11 pm on the 28th south of the Robertson River Bridge off the Alaska Highway (zone 224), and continued through October 31st.  A total of 13.2 inches of snow had fallen by 4 pm on the 29th, with a storm total of 20 inches by 9 am on the 31st.  Based on the observations from the NWS Storm Spotter south of the Robertson River Bridge, about 12 miles west of Tanacross, it is very likely that similar or greater amounts of snow fell along parts of the north facing slopes in the Eastern Alaska Range.  A cooperative observer at Dry Creek reported a storm total of 11.3 inches from this event.

A significant amount of water and ice combined with ice jams in the vicinity of the village as well as downriver produced flooding in the village of Emmonak. The flooding was compounded by 2 to 5 miles of shore-fast ice at the mouth of the Yukon River. The water levels rose during the early morning hours on the 23rd, and started to flood parts of the village as well as the road to the airport. By the afternoon of the 23rd, water inundated many of the low lying structures. The water levels continued to rise, and power was shut off to the village during the morning of the 23rd. By the evening of the 24th, there were evacuations of people living in low lying areas. Several homes along the river were flooded with 1 to 3 feet of water. The water levels began to fall later in the evening, and were back within bank full by the late evening hours of the 25th or the early morning hours on the 26th. Concerning property damage, According to the Arctic Sounder weekly newspaper, as of August 27, Emmonak filed with the State of AK and FEMA for about $410,000 in housing assistance (79 applicatnts) and $491,600 for other needs (87 applicants).

Dense smoke was widespread in Fairbanks and North Pole on the morning of August 8, 2009, and the visibility was one quarter mile or less at the Fairbanks International Airport. Locally the visibility was reduced to only a couple hundred yards. The dense smoke was due to several large wildfires that were burning across interior Alaska. Although the smoke was dense on the morning of the 8th, areas of smoke reduced the visibility to one mile or less at times from August 1-7, 2009. The thick smoke brought air traffic in and out of Fairbanks to a standstill on August 5th, when most of the 30 plus flights that were scheduled to arrive or depart from Fairbanks International Airport were either cancelled or indefinitely delayed.  The smoke resulted in very poor air quality, and at times, the air quality was above the hazardous level. The combination of a strong frontal passage and rain finally cleared out the smoke on the morning of the 9th. Several sports activities were cancelled during this time including a Senior Games tennis tournament and the High School Football Jamboree.
A funnel cloud was observed on the FAA web camera from the Bethel airport to the southeast of Bethel the evening of September 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This funnel cloud was captured on the FAA Web camera from the Bethel Airport. Position of the funnel cloud is estimated from the web images and the Bethel WSR-88D radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Hard Luck Creek Fire was a human caused fire that started on August 3, 2009, and was located between Murphy Dome and the Chatanika River approximately 20 miles northwest of Fairbanks. The fire burned a total of 12,834 acres and destroyed one home and two cabins. The fire was contained on the evening of August 16, 2009.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped below freezing on the morning of September 28th under clear skies and calm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yakutat observed the first hard freeze of the season on the morning of September 29th under clear skies on calm winds. Temperatures ranged below 28F from 0500 to 0700 AKST with a minimum of 26F. This freeze ended the growing season for the Yakutat area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1050 mb high centered several hundred miles north of the arctic coast combined with a 984 mb low across the eastern Aleutians and western Gulf of Alaska to produce a strong pressure gradient along the arctic and Chukchi Sea coasts.  The strong wind produced significant blowing and drifting snow. Although little or no snow fell during this event, the visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less in blowing snow at Deadhorse and at Point Hope.  The worst conditions were observed at Point Hope where there were occasional white-out conditions, and the wind frequently gusted over 50 mph, with occasional gusts to around 60 mph.  A peak wind gust of 56 kt/64 mph was observed at the Point Hope AWOS.  The wind at Deadhorse was not as strong, but gusted to 41kt/47 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The minimum temperature on 10/12 for the Hyder was 29F during the early morning hours. The estimated temperatures for outlying areas were in the lower 20s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 979 mb low in the eastern Yukon Territory on the morning of the 18th weakened and moved east during the day.  The low produced a period of stronger wind along the Steese Highway summits. A west wind of 25 to 35 mph combined with temperatures of 25 to 30 below to produce wind chills as low as 65 below.  The strongest wind and lowest wind chills were observed at Eagle Summit.
A 992 mb low north of Demarcation Point brought a period of high winds to Bartler Island during the morning hours on the 3rd.  A peak wind gust of 57kt/66 mph was observed at the Barter Island AWOS.

A 952 mb low in the southern Bering Sea combined with a 1060 mb high across the Yukon to produce strong winds and blizzard conditions at Gambell from the late afternoon hours on the 4th through the early morning hours on the 5th.  The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow.  The Gambell AWOS had sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with occasional gusts as high as 47 mph/41kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 955 mb near Nome at 9 am on the 11th tracked across the Seward Peninsula and slowly weakened to 982 mb near Selawik by 3 pm on the 12th. The low brought a moist westerly flow into the interior, and produced heavy snowfall in the Upper Koyukuk Valley, parts of the Central Interior, and across the higher elevations in the Middle Tanana Valley around Fairbanks. Here are some snowfall reports that were received from this event: ||Zone 219: Heavy snow fell in Bettles with a storm total of 25.3 inches (three day total from the 11th through the 13th).  The heaviest snow fell on the 12th, with a 24 hour total of 14.2 inches.||Zone 220: Blizzard conditions likely occurred along parts of the Steese Highway in the vicinity of Eagle Summit from the late afternoon hours on the 12th through the late morning hours on the 13th.  According to a statement from the State of Alaska DOT, travel was not advised on the Steese Highway MP 101 to MP 121.  Strong winds, heavy snow and drifting snow were reported with low to zero visibility. ||Zone 221: Heavy snow fell in parts of the Central Interior.  The Manley DOT observed a total of 11 inches of snow in Manley, and estimated that one to two feet of snow fell over the higher elevations along the Elliot Highway.||Zone 222: Heavy snow fell on the hills around Fairbanks with storm totals of 9 to 15 inches. In the valleys storm totals were generally in the 2 to 6 inch range. Here are a few of the higher snowfall totals that were observed from this event: ||Keystone Ridge: 15.5 inches|Mile 42 Steese Highway: 12.0 inches|Fort knox Mine: 9.0 inches.

A 1050 mb high centered several hundred miles north of the arctic coast combined with a 984 mb low across the eastern Aleutians and western Gulf of Alaska to produce a strong pressure gradient along the arctic and Chukchi Sea coasts.  The strong wind produced significant blowing and drifting snow. Although little or no snow fell during this event, the visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less in blowing snow at Deadhorse and at Point Hope.  The worst conditions were observed at Point Hope where there were occasional white-out conditions, and the wind frequently gusted over 50 mph, with occasional gusts to around 60 mph.  A peak wind gust of 56 kt/64 mph was observed at the Point Hope AWOS.  The wind at Deadhorse was not as strong, but gusted to 41kt/47 mph.

A 980 mb low near Saint Lawrence Island at 9 pm on the 10th deepened rapidly to 955 mb near Nome by 9 am on the 11th. The low then tracked across the Seward Peninsula and slowly weakened to 982 mb near Selawik by 3 pm on the 12th. Strong west winds developed across the Yukon Delta during the afternoon of the 11th to the south of the low. The high winds were accompanied by snow, and the visibility was reduced to one quarter to one half mile at times. Here are the peak wind gusts that were observed during the late afternoon and early evening on the 11th: ||Emmonak AWOS: 58 kt/67 mph|Mountain Village AWSS: 50kt/58 mph.

A 980 mb low near Saint Lawrence Island at 9 pm on the 10th deepened rapidly to 955 mb near Nome by 9 am on the 11th. The low then tracked across the Seward Peninsula and slowly weakened to 982 mb near Selawik by 3 pm on the 12th. Strong west winds in the wake of the low caused the water levels to rise 8 to 10 feet across the eastern Norton Sound. There were reports of minor flooding in low lying areas in Unalakleet during the evening of the 11th into the early morning hours on the 12th. Several homes on the south side of the village near the mouth of the Unalakleet River had 1 to 3 feet around them as water splashed over the gabions, but no water entered the homes as they were constructed on pilings. At the airport, there was some spray that hit buildings and there were logs on the west side of the runway that had to be cleared. The marine navigation beacon was broken and many of the river markers were gone in the wake of the storm. Along the Yukon Delta Coast, there was minor damage to fuel heads at the fuel tanks at Scammon Bay.

An strong front pushed through the south central region of Alaska that produced high wind along Turnagain Arm and the Anchorage hillside. Mchugh Creek and Glenn Alps reported wind gusts of 81 and 82 mph. This front pushed ample moisture up into south central Alaska that produced heavy snow in the Susitna Valley. Chulitna reported a storm total of 16 inches of snow with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms along the Chugach Mountains near Anchorage produced a funnel cloud near Wolverine Peak around 6 PM AKDT May 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person observed the funnel cloud and took pictures of it above Wolverine Peak the evening of May 26th.
